title: The Tunnel Crew
desc: New tunnels don't dig themselves. Neither do they shore themselves.

steps.text:
Expansion never stops in Mount Pillar. The hive grows, the population grows, and the tunnels must grow to match. Today's project: a new residential corridor connecting the mid-levels to the Queen's Gate approach. Sixty feet of solid rock, eight feet wide, seven feet high.

The Mandiblor supervising the crew is built like the tunnels she digs — solid, square, and immovable. She chalks the cutting line on the rock face.

"Standard bore. Follow the chalk. Cutting team at the face, shoring team two body-lengths behind. Nobody works ahead of the shores. I don't care how fast you can dig — unsupported rock kills."

She hands you a cutting tool — a hardened chisel with a weighted handle, designed for percussive splitting. "You cut. I'll read the stone."

options.label: Cut the face — follow the chalk line
Join the shoring team — structural work

steps.text:
Cutting is percussion. You strike the chisel point along the chalk line, following the grain of the rock. The stone splits in sheets — Mount Pillar's native rock is layered sediment, and if you read the grain correctly, whole slabs peel away cleanly.

The supervisor reads the stone for you. "Angle left. The grain shifts here — see the color change? Hit it straight and it'll fracture wrong. Angle left and the slab drops clean."

You angle left. The slab drops clean — a two-hundred-pound sheet of rock that crashes to the tunnel floor and is immediately hauled away by the debris crew. Behind it: more rock, but softer, easier to work.

Three hours of cutting produces eight feet of new tunnel. The shoring crew follows behind, installing wooden frames and stone keystones. The fresh tunnel smells like broken rock and ambition.

The supervisor inspects the face. "Good grain-reading. You lost the line at the six-foot mark — see where the chalk goes straight but your cut curves? That's you following the grain instead of the plan." She re-chalks the line. "The grain is a guide, not a boss. The plan comes first."

You file the lesson. In Mount Pillar, everything serves the plan. Even the stone.

options.label: Rest and prepare for the next cutting session

steps.text:
The shoring team works two body-lengths behind the cutters, installing support frames as fast as the tunnel advances. It's a race — the rock above the fresh cut is unsupported, and every minute without shores is a minute where gravity remembers it has opinions.

The frames are pre-cut wood — harvested from the lower root systems and shaped to standard tunnel dimensions. You lift them into position, wedge them tight against the ceiling, and lock them with keystones — specially cut stone blocks that transfer the ceiling's weight to the frame and down to the floor.

The first three frames go in clean. The fourth is trouble — the ceiling here isn't flat. A bulge of harder rock protrudes, and the standard frame doesn't seat flush. A gap between frame and ceiling means a failure point.

You improvise. You chisel the bulge flat — quick work with a hand tool, shaping the ceiling to meet the frame rather than the other way around. Chips of hard rock rain down. The frame seats flush. The keystone locks.

The supervisor checks your work. She pushes on the frame. It doesn't move. She nods — the highest praise available.

"The bulge was ironstone — harder than the surrounding sediment. Good call shaping the ceiling. Most newbies try to reshape the frame." She marks the ironstone deposit on the tunnel map. "The miners will want to know about that. Ironstone has value."

Your shoring holds. The tunnel advances. Mount Pillar grows by eight more feet today.

options.label: Admire the new tunnel and its solid shores
